single-choice-1.title: Purpose of aggregate functions
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the main purpose of aggregate functions like COUNT() in SQL?
single-choice-1.hint: Aggregate functions produce a single summary value.

help.concept: Aggregate functions like COUNT(), SUM(), and AVG() are used to summarize or combine information from multiple rows into a single result.
help.hint_1: COUNT() gives you a total, not a list of rows.
help.hint_2: Aggregate functions are for summarizing, not for updating or deleting rows.

options.a: To summarize data across multiple rows
options.b: To update every row in a table
options.d: To filter rows based on a condition

multi-choice-1.title: When does COUNT(*) return a different result than COUNT(column)?
multi-choice-1.prompt: Select all scenarios where COUNT(*) and COUNT(notes) would return different results on the inventory_items table.
multi-choice-1.hint: Think about how NULL values affect COUNT(column).

help.concept: COUNT(*) counts all rows. COUNT(column) only counts rows where the column is not NULL. If some or all rows have NULL in the column, the counts will differ.
help.hint_1: If there are NULLs in 'notes', COUNT(notes) will be less than COUNT(*).
help.hint_2: If every row has NULL in 'notes', COUNT(notes) will be zero, but COUNT(*) will still count all rows.

options.a: Some rows have NULL in the 'notes' column
options.b: All rows have a value in the 'notes' column
options.c: No rows have a value in the 'notes' column
options.d: The 'notes' column is always NOT NULL

sketch-1.title: Counting Rows with COUNT()
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
The `COUNT()` function in SQL is used to count the number of rows that match a specified condition. For example, to count all items in the `inventory_items` table:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM inventory_items;
```

You can also count only non-null values in a specific column:

```sql
SELECT COUNT(name) FROM inventory_items;
```

This counts only rows where `name` is not NULL.

sketch-2.title: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(column)
sketch-2.bodyMarkdown: `COUNT(*)` counts all rows, including those with NULLs in any column. `COUNT(column)` only counts rows where the specified column is not NULL. In most business tables, columns like `id` or `name` are usually NOT NULL, so both forms often return the same result, but not always.
